text: Catalytic borylation of C-H bonds yields highly selectively the desired products. Thus, activation of primary and secondary C-H bonds that are assisted by electron withdrawing groups is observed in good to excellent yield while the catalytic reaction at a tertiary C-H bond leads to no product. Here we report that an iridium catalyst could convert selectively the bridgehead (tertiary) C-H bond to give quantitative amounts of bridgehead B-pinacoyl or B-methoxy esters. It was also demonstrated that over 35 substrates yielded the boronated product with good to excellent yield and more than 95% selectivity. Catalysts variants were also used to late stage modify with success on known drugs with the sought bridgehead junction and more generally to prepare novel bicyclic core building blocks. Kinetic and NMR/DFT studies elucidate the mechanism of the reaction. Cleavage of the C-H bond presents a modest activation energy; the turnover limiting step is an isomerization reaction prior to reductive coupling.
headline: Fully Human Written
